content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 22, 1976


Mr. Kenneth W. Perkins, Jr.
Acting City Attorney
City of California City
9501 California City Boulevard
California City, California 93505

Dear Mr. Perkins:

This is in response to your letter of August 12, 1976,
concerning the possible existence of a conflict of interest on
the part of two members of the City Council of California City
when dealing with matters involving Great Western Cities, Inc.
(hereafter "Great Western"). Although the following advice is in-
formal, I trust it will serve to resolve your questions concern-
ing this matter.

Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 provide, in
relevant part:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest.

§87100

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material finan-
cial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the pub-
lic generally, on: ...

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a com-
mercial lending institution in the regular course of
business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; ...

§87103(c)

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5601 · ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-5661

Mr. Kenneth W. Perkins, Jr.
September 22, 1976
Page 2

The spouses of the two councilmen are employees of a
hotel which is a wholly owned subsidiary of Great Western. Thus,
we think it is clear that Great Western is a source of income to
the spouses of the two councilmen in question, and hence, by virtue of their community property interest in such income, also a
source of income to the councilmen. See Government Code Section
82030. Accordingly, the officials involved would be required to
disqualify themselves where the criteria defining "financial interest" in Government Code Section 87103 are found to exist.
However, with respect to any particular decision which any councilmen may make or in which they may participate which would involve Great Western (examples of which have not been provided),
these criteria may not be present. In particular, many decisions
probably will not have a "material financial effect" on Great
Western.

The Commission's regulation defining the phrase "material financial effect," 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, states, in
relevant part:

(b) In determining the existence of a material effect
upon a financial interest, consideration should be given,
but not limited to, an analysis of the following factors:
...

(3) In the case of a source of income of a public official as defined in Government Code Section 87103(c):

(A) The decision will affect the source of income 1/
in the manner described in subsection (b)(1) above;1/

(B) Whether the governmental decision will directly affect the amount of income to be received by
the official;

(C) Whether there is a nexus between the governmental decision and the purpose for which the official receives income.

While we cannot anticipate, absent factual examples, the types of
decisions involving Great Western which are likely to come before
the City Council, it appears that consideration of the factors
articulated in subsections (B) and (C), cited above, often will
result in a finding that the general concept of "materiality"
stated in the regulation is not present. Under these circumstances, disqualification of the councilmen based on the fact
that Great Western is a source of income will not be required.

1/Subsection (b)(1) specifies certain dollar amounts and percentages which can be used as guidelines in assessing the materiality of the financial impact of the decision on the business entity.

Mr. Kenneth W. Perkins, Jr.
September 22, 1976
Page 3

With respect to your question concerning the applicability of Government Code Section 1090 et seq. to the councilmen
in question, I initially must state that the Fair Political Practices Commission does not have jurisdiction to issue opinions on
sections of the Government Code other than the Political Reform
Act. Accordingly, you may wish to request an opinion of the Attorney General on this specific matter. However, we would direct
your attention to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which provides:

...(1) An officer or employee shall not be deemed to
be interested in a contract if his interest is:

...(f) That of a spouse of an officer or employee
of a public agency in his spouse's employment or
officeholding if his spouse's employment or officeholding has existed for at least one year prior to
his election or appointment.

Thank you for writing concerning this matter. If you
have further questions please do not hesitate to call me at
(916)322-6444.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist

KWG:jo

City of California City

Incorporated 1965

City Hall

PHONE (714) 373-8861

9501 CALIFORNIA CITY BLVD - CALIFORNIA CITY, CALIFORNIA 93505

[SEAL: City of California City seal]

[RECEIVED stamp with date]

August 12, 1976


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca.  95804


Gentlemen:

The purpose of this letter is to request an opinion as
to a possible conflict of interest as the result of the
employment of spouses of two of our City Councilmen.
The first Councilman is the Mayor, Mr. Donald Conley,
and the second Councilman is a Councilman by the name
of Glenn Metzger.

The spouses of each of these Councilmen are employed by
a hotel which is situated within the city limits.  Mr.
Conley's wife has the position of Director of Public
Relations.  Mr. Metzger's wife is employed as a cook in
the kitchen.  The hotel is a wholly-owned subsidiary of
Great Western Cities, Inc.  Great Western Cities, Inc.
was the original developer and subdivider of the City
and it and it's legal predecessors were in existence and
developing the City prior to the incorporation of the
City.  To date, Great Western Cities, Inc. is still a
major land holder within the City and has several
contracts with the City dealing with obligations arising
out of the original subdivision maps and development.

Mrs. Conley's position includes responsibilities for
supervising purchasing for the hotel.  The purchasing
extends only to supplies and inventory and she has no
control over capital improvements.  She, in turn, is
supervised by the General Manager of the hotel and is
an employee of the hotel only.

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page Two
August 12, 1976


Mrs. Metzger has responsibilities only as a cook and has
no supervisory or purchasing responsibility.

The questions presented are:

1. Do the terms of Proposition 9 require disqualification on the part of either Mr. Conley or Mr. Metzger
when dealing with a matter in which Great Western
Cities, Inc. is involved.

2. Do the terms of Government Code Sections 1090, et
seq., require disqualification of either Mr. Conley
or Mr. Metzger when dealing with contracts with Great
Western Cities, Inc.

Thank you for your courtesy and attention.

Yours truly,

[signature]
Kenneth W. Perkins, Jr.
Acting City Attorney

KWP/Fb

tion on the part of either Mr. Conley or Mr. Metzger

Western Cities, Inc.

Yours truly,

sections.conclusion: Great Western is a source of income to the spouses of the two councilmen in question, and hence, by virtue of community property interest in such income, also a source of income to the councilmen. The officials involved would be required to disqualify themselves from decisions meeting criteria in Government Code Section 87103. However, many decisions will not have a 'material financial effect' on Great Western and thus may not trigger disqualification requirements. Regarding Section 1090, the FPPC does not have jurisdiction to issue opinions on sections of the Government Code other than the Political Reform Act. However, attention is drawn to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which provides an exemption for the interest of a spouse's employment or business if the spouse's interest is not a material financial interest.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether two city councilmen whose spouses are employed by a hotel subsidiary of Great Western Cities, Inc. must disqualify themselves from decisions involving Great Western under Proposition 9 and Government Code Sections 1090 et seq.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Great Western constitutes a source of income to the councilmen through their spouses' employment, requiring disqualification analysis under Political Reform Act Section 87103 when decisions have material financial effects on Great Western. The FPPC lacks jurisdiction over Government Code Section 1090, but directs attention to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which may provide an exemption for spouse employment interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality affects readability; document contains both incoming request (August 12, 1976) and FPPC response (September 22, 1976). Some text fragmented or unclear due to OCR errors. FPPC declines jurisdiction on Section 1090 question. Document references Government Code sections 87100, 2030, 87103, 1872, 1090 et seq., and 1091.5(1)(f).

CONCLUSION: Great Western is a source of income to the spouses of the two councilmen in question, and hence, by virtue of community property interest in such income, also a source of income to the councilmen. The officials involved would be required to disqualify themselves from decisions meeting criteria in Government Code Section 87103. However, many decisions will not have a 'material financial effect' on Great Western and thus may not trigger disqualification requirements. Regarding Section 1090, the FPPC does not have jurisdiction to issue opinions on sections of the Government Code other than the Political Reform Act. However, attention is drawn to Section 1091.5(1)(f) which provides an exemption for the interest of a spouse's employment or business if the spouse's interest is not a material financial interest.

embedding.summary: An FPPC advice letter responding to a city's inquiry regarding potential conflicts of interest where two councilmen have spouses employed by a hotel subsidiary of Great Western Cities, Inc., a major developer and contractor with the city. The FPPC concludes that Great Western is an income source to the councilmen through community property rights, requiring disqualification on decisions with material financial effects, while noting jurisdictional limitations regarding Government Code Section 1090.
